sentences.text: I can answer you there to the best of my ability.
Asael was working, sort of I guess-- a village also has a city hall, if you will.
And he was the chairman of the city hall.
This was one excuse, I would say.
Then, like five or 10 years back before the war, a Gentile fellow killed a Gentile neighbor.
And this was thrown on my brothers, on Zisl and Asael, that they killed that neighbor.
But it was not true.
Asael and Zisl never touched those people.
So I would end up-- before the war started, you have to understand that to be a Jewish villager, to live in the village for many years to grow up, it wasn't so simple or easy.
Maybe you have seen the musical-- what was it?
At times, they show you how the police were terrorizing and neighbors were terrorizing the Jewish people, the Jewish village.
But us, they would never terrorize as soon as my brothers grew up.
I guess that was luck, or that's the way it's had to be.
They were very tough young men, unique.
And they took care of the whole family actually.
Those two, Asael and Zisl, mainly.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] After a couple of weeks into the war, my parents told the police and the German soldiers that they are in the Russian army, both.
So my father was totally beat up and he had to lay in bed, was totally beat up.
They broke every bone in his body.
I beg your pardon?
These did-- mainly, I saw what it was like.
I was hidden and I-- but I saw the policeman did the hitting, not the German soldiers.
Policeman did the hitting.
And at that time, Abraham and Yankel were at home.
They took them to the city.
They arrested them.
And my father was laying flat on his back in bed, could not walk, nothing.
He was totally beat up.
And then, my mother, some way, somehow it got to my mother after two or three weeks that if she has an X amount of gold to buy-- to give to the--
Excuse me.
She took her own, whatever she had, and she borrowed from other people gold.
And me and her went to Nowogrodek, to Nowogrodek to get those two brothers out of jail.
We went with a horse and buggy.
I remember we left the horse and buggy at my uncle in Nowogrodek.
And my mother was going to a Jewish man to bring him the gold so he can give to the chief of the police to let my two brothers out.
But those days already, the Jews cannot walk on the sidewalk.
And they also had yellow mark.
A yellow star.
Correct.
I did not have one.
My mother did.
She walked with the street in the city.
I had no yellow star.
I don't know why.
Maybe I wasn't afraid.
I don't know.
I walked with the sidewalk, followed her like a distance of like a half-- like a Manhattan city block away.
Some way, somehow, a policeman recognized me in the city-- it was about 12:00 during the day-- recognize me in the city.
Within moments, there were Nazi soldiers with him.
And they arrested me, took me to the police station.
They took me to the police station.
On the way into the police station, they took my two brothers, that they were arrested-- this was the last time I have seen them.
They took them from the police station to the jail, where the big jail is.
This was the last time I saw them.
They brought me to the police station.
They took me in the back.
Was about six or seven German soldiers.
They surrounded me.
They gave me a shovel to dig a hole in the ground.

And while I was digging the hole in the ground that was for myself-- --they were manipulating with the rifles and threatened if I wouldn't give them the information where Asael and Zisl is, they will kill me.
This was my first encounter.
You asked me before when I've seen the Nazis.
This was my very first and last-- --encounter with the-- with the Nazis, that they had their hand over me.
They made me-- it was rocky ground-- to dig the hole with my fingers.
They said I should dig it with my teeth.
Cannot do that.
I digged about a foot, foot and a half deep.
They made me lay down if it's big enough.
Finally, they took me inside the police station and put me behind bars.
There was sitting-- there were arrested some Gentile White Russian people.
And they said, you Jewish, you're finished.
You'll never get out of here.
I guess after two or three hours, maybe, the very same Germans that brought me to the police station, they came, took me out from behind bars.
Go home.
Why they let me go home?
I couldn't tell you that.
I couldn't answer that.
You couldn't ask no questions.
This was the only encounter I had with the Nazis that they had their head-- their hand over me.
I came home late in the evening, and my mother was home.
And my father was, as I said, beat up.
And somewheres after a couple of weeks, the-- two or three weeks, the war started.
Zisl and Asael came home.
Did not succeed to buy them out, because she did not have enough.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] So later on, they were butchered with the other thousands of Jews that they killed.
Outskirts of Novogrudok.
So obvious, my mother told them that the cops and the Nazis are looking for them.
So during the day, they would hide in the woods.
They were only come at night to get food.
And this is the way life went on, I guess, till somewhere towards the end of October.
We were constantly terrorized, day in, day out.
As I said, it's hard to-- I'm not capable enough to bring you across what we lived through, the terrorism of the Gentile police.
Not every neighbor.
Not all the neighbors.
Just some of them.
But they will always come--
With Nazis. --know that your brothers were coming home at night?
Hard to tell.
Hard to say that.
I don't think so.
At those days, no.
[DRINKS]
New people.
New people.
Totally new, that they were glad that they were liberated from the Russians and the Nazis came, in particular started to kill Jews.
From the same area.
The one that reported me when I told you before the story, that I was arrested, taken to the police station.
Nazis didn't know me.
One of the neighbors, he was Polish, right away he became a policeman.
And he pointed me out to the Nazis, that this is the youngest brother of two bad Jewish men, I guess.
Kuszel.
Wasja.
Wasja.
It was a Polish family.
Coming home only at night for a few minutes just to take with them sandwiches.
And then, they would go back to the woods.
Then, one day-- it was on a Thursday, I think.
It was on a Thursday afternoon.
It started to snow, late October.
I was feeding the horse and the cow, because I was the only kid at home for the night.
I was walking with-- in the mill, in the stable.
All of a sudden, I heard like, a noise of an airplane or a truck.
And it stopped.
I turn around to the mill.
I was at a distance, maybe like Manhattan block, a city block away.
I saw Nazi soldiers jumping down the truck on the bridge of our mill.
So I dropped everything, and I escaped.
I went to the woods.
I knew roughly where my brothers are.
I did not know exactly.
But I knew roughly, if I go there and there, I will find them.
I will wait, or they will find me.
I'll cross them.
So I went about four or five kilometers-- it was already dark-- to another [NON-ENGLISH], if you will, Gentile family.
And there were my brothers, Asael and Zisl.

As I said, when the trucks-- the Nazis came with the truck, arrested my parents, and that fellow that grew up in our family, this one, Adolf Stishok, he worked for us, got married while he was living with us.
His wife and he had a son.
Everything.
When my mother was taken to the truck with my father, my mother asked him for a pair of galoshes she forgot to take.
So he was laughing at her.
And said, where you going, you don't need galoshes.
Sarcastically.
Then, in the evening, when I found my two brothers, Asael and Zisl, I told them this story.
So the first thing what they did was they went back to our village, to Stankiewicze, and left me with one family.
With one Gentile family in Stankiewicze, which their name is, as I said before, Shikas.
They left me there-- --overnight and over the day, because we had a sister about 10 kilometers away, married to another Jewish farmer.
And it was a whole family.
She had her daughter, six months old, her husband, her in-laws.
Was a whole Jewish family.
Toby.
So they left me with this Gentile family.
And they went to get my sister and her all family out, because my parents were arrested, taken to the city.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] During the day, the very same policeman that came with the Nazis a week or two weeks before was coming.
He was a lot of times in our-- terrorized us.
The same policeman came to this family where I was in the kitchen.
He was in the living room.
And he said to this Gentile family, he said, the little one escaped yesterday.
Maybe he's hiding in your house.
Maybe you're hiding him.
So there were two brothers and a sister.
They [? answered, ?] they would not hide any Jews.
He is not here.
I was sitting in a kitchen, and this was in the living room.
I would run away to the back house, but he had-- they had a very mean dog that could tear apart a person.
So I was afraid of the dog.
I had to sit it out in the kitchen.
And I did.
And they did not give me out.
He was sitting maybe half an hour, an hour.
And he left.
Then, in evening--
Well, the only way I could describe it, I guess, is bad news.
[LAUGHS] I don't know if a kid in my-- I knew one thing, though.
If the policeman is there, I have to run the opposite, have to hide.
But there is-- was no way out.
So you just have to sit it out, I guess.
I don't think I understood the real-- the real fear, the real consequences if you get caught.
But I guess instinctly, to hide.
But they did not give me out.
In the evening, came my brothers.
And from there and on, I was never left in this area, in this immediate our-- around our village.
If I was left, it was already farther away, like 10 or 20 kilometers away.
Never left with a neighbor.
And those neighbors, obviously, were very nice people.
They were good people.
White Russians.
They could very easily give me away to the-- to him.
And if they would get caught that they hiding a Jew, their life and their farm isn't worth a nickel.
They would get burned.
Killed and burned, the entire family, for helping a Jewish kid or whoever.
So they actually put their life totally at stake.
And a lot of those people, White Russian, did get hurt from the Nazis.
A lot of them got killed for helping out Jews.
Well, in our area, yes.
From this area, they were helping us.
If you helped us, later on, we were together, like 1,200 Jews got together.
It's a-- it's a town.
Mainly at those days-- this was the very first winter into the war-- mainly--
Winter of 1941.
Mainly those were people on good terms, and not on bad.
I'd always-- two friends, two good people to come.
Can I leave my brother here for a week or three days?
This is the way it continued.
But don't forget, we already had-- this time, we already had about 15 people to take care of.
Except us.
Except me and my two brothers.
The family of my sister, and we were spread out by-- to different Gentile people.
And this is the way we pushed through the first winter.
[SIGHS] Some way, somehow, there was a Gentile fellow in another village.
His name was Kozlovskii.
He spoke Yiddish as well as I did.
He used to go often to the city.
And he was the one-- --that brought word to the Jews in Nowogrodek, particularly to our relatives that we had in Nowogrodek.
He brought word that Asael and Zisl is alive, and I am alive.
And if they want to come out from this city, run away from the ghetto, they could come to his house.
And my brothers would go to his house and pick them up.
They started of spring, 1942.
They started, some way, somehow, in-- there was no snow already.
Like, May, June.
We had a cousin.
We had many cousins.
But one of them came out with his wife.
He brought out with them a fellow who just died, Pesach Frydberg.
Yudel.
Julius Bielski.
He's dead.
He passed away here in New York.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]
So what they-- actually happened was that all our relatives did run away from the ghetto to us.
And they brought friends with them, mainly young men.
And I guess the first summer in 1942, we probably had like 50, 60 people [? to ?] my brothers had to worry about 'em.
And this was the beginning of the partisans, if you will.
Woods.
In the woods.
Under the sky, under trees.
[LAUGHS] They would build, build a sort of, if you want to call it, a hut or a little room under the ground.
[NON-ENGLISH].
I don't know if there is a English word for that.
Well, first of all, gets you out of the-- out of the cold.
It's mainly for the winter.
And you know, it's-- you can lay down to sleep on it.
In it.
It's a hole in the ground, but a big one.
Enough for three, or four, or five people.
In that hole in the ground.
They had blankets, whatever.
Of course.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We slept-- the second winter, the winter of 1942, they chased us out from one place of the woods into another.
In other words, some way, somehow, it got-- we could never stay a long time in one place.
You had to be constantly on the move.
It got to the Nazis where we at in the winter of 1942.
Was in the height of the winter.
I think it was in already in 1943, like January or February, because it was the height of the winter.
And three weeks, we were sleeping on snow.
Yeah. 50, 60 people.
Who was in charge?
It's hard to tell, exactly.
[INAUDIBLE] It was January.
I know it was at the height of the winter of '42 that my brother, Tuvia, came to us in our area.
He was for about six months, seven months under-- living under a Gentile name.
And he was mixing with the Nazis, ate with them, with the soldiers.
